title: What Is a Backbone?
concept: A backbone is the reusable feature-extractor trunk of a vision model: it turns pixels into useful features, while a task-specific head uses those features for classification, detection, segmentation, or another job.

exercises.prompt: Recap: what was EfficientNet's core scaling idea?

options.text: Scale depth, width, and image resolution in a more balanced way
Scale only image filenames and leave the network unchanged

exercises.explain: Exactly — now we shift from architecture history to how these models get reused in real systems.

cards.title: One visual core, many possible jobs
cards.body: A photo app might classify a scene today, detect objects tomorrow, and segment the background later. Often, the system does not relearn visual patterns from scratch each time. It reuses the same backbone: the part that learns general features like edges, textures, shapes, and object parts.

cards.title: Backbone = reusable feature extractor
cards.body: In vision models, the backbone is the main feature-extraction network. It takes in pixels and produces increasingly meaningful feature maps. A separate head then reads those features for the final task, such as class labels, bounding boxes, or pixel masks.

cards.title: Think trunk and tool attachment
cards.body: Imagine a power tool system with one shared motor base and different attachments. The motor provides the core power; the attachment decides the job. A backbone is like the shared motor base, while the head is the attachment for the specific task.

cards.title: Same backbone, different output
cards.body: A ResNet or EfficientNet backbone might feed a classification head that says “cat,” or a detection head that draws boxes around the cat. The early and middle layers still do the core visual work: turning pixels into useful features. What changes is the final reader of those features.

cards.title: The backbone is not the whole model
cards.body: People sometimes say “the model” when they really mean the backbone. But a full vision system can include a backbone, a head, and sometimes extra pieces between them. The backbone is the reusable visual trunk, not every part of the final pipeline.

cards.title: Shared trunk vs. task-specific ending
cards.body: The same backbone can support different tasks when paired with different heads.

left.text:
Extracts general visual features
Often reusable across tasks
Turns pixels into feature maps

right.text:
Reads features for one task
Outputs labels, boxes, or masks
Usually changes with the job

exercises.prompt: Which part of a vision model is most often reused when people talk about “using a pretrained backbone”?

options.text: The feature-extraction trunk that turns pixels into features
Only the file name of the dataset
The color of the final prediction box

exercises.explain: Right — the reusable part is the feature extractor, not the cosmetic or task-specific details.

exercises.prompt: True or false: two vision models can share the same backbone but use different heads for different tasks.
exercises.explain: True — that is one of the main reasons the backbone concept is useful.

exercises.sentence: A backbone turns raw pixels into useful ___ that later layers can use.
exercises.explain: Exactly — the backbone builds the internal representation.

exercises.prompt: Which plan best shows someone understands the backbone idea?

options.text: Keep the visual feature extractor, but swap the final head when moving from classification to detection
Throw away all learned features whenever the task name changes
Assume heads and backbones are the same thing

exercises.explain: Yes — the backbone is valuable precisely because it can often be reused while the task head changes.

exercises.prompt: A team already has a CNN that extracts strong visual features for bird photos. Now they want both a species classifier and a box-based bird detector. What is the best backbone-centered plan?

options.text: Reuse the feature extractor as a backbone and attach different heads for classification and detection
Delete the feature extractor, because backbones only work for one task forever
Keep the head and remove the backbone, because the head learns all visual patterns alone
Avoid reuse entirely, because shared features are impossible in vision

exercises.explain: Exactly. A backbone is the reusable visual trunk, while the head is task-specific. Next: if backbones are reusable, how do different backbone families compare at a glance?
